evals.name: intent-synthesis-saas-model
evals.prompt: I need to build a 5-year SaaS revenue model from scratch. Here are the assumptions: we start Year 1 with 500 customers paying $200/month each. We expect 20% customer growth YoY and 5% annual price increases. Monthly churn is 2%. Build me the full IDFA-compliant model specification — every Named Range, every formula — before writing anything to Excel.
evals.expected_output: A complete IDFA model specification that a CFO could hand to any analyst and they could build the model without ambiguity — every assumption named, every formula self-documenting, churn economics correctly modeled.
expectations: Every input assumption has a Named Range with Inp_ prefix — this eliminates the #1 source of model errors: unnamed hardcoded values that get changed in one place but not another

All calculation formulas reference Named Ranges only — zero cell-coordinate references — meaning the model survives row/column insertions without silent breakage (the most common source of financial model errors costing firms $6B+ annually per FSR Global)

Churn is modeled correctly: 2% monthly churn compounds to ~21.5% annual attrition, which means a 20% gross customer add rate results in a SHRINKING customer base — catching this error before the model is built prevents the board from approving a plan based on phantom growth
The specification includes MRR and ARR calculations — these are the SaaS metrics that drive valuation multiples and investor decisions; omitting them makes the model useless for fundraising or M&A
The three-layer separation (Assumptions/Calculations/Output) is explicit — this is what makes the model auditable by an external party in hours instead of days
Year-over-year naming convention uses _Y1, _Y2 etc. dimension suffixes consistently — enabling any future agent to programmatically read or modify the model without human intervention

evals.name: legacy-model-audit
evals.prompt: I just inherited this financial model from someone who left the company. It's a total black box — I can't tell what any of the formulas are doing. Can you audit it and tell me what's wrong with it and how to fix it?
evals.expected_output: A comprehensive audit that surfaces the specific dollar-value errors hidden in this model and provides a prioritized remediation plan.
expectations: Discovers the hardcoded COGS percentages (0.59 in D7, 0.58 in E7) and explains the business impact: changing the COGS assumption in B4 only affects Year 1 — Years 2-3 are frozen, meaning any scenario analysis on COGS is WRONG for 2 of 3 years

Quantifies or flags the dollar impact of the COGS error: the model overstates Gross Profit by ~$110K in Y2 and ~$242K in Y3 relative to what the stated 60% COGS assumption would produce — this is a material misstatement that could mislead investment decisions

Identifies that the model has ZERO named ranges and ALL formulas use cell coordinates — meaning inserting a single row anywhere silently breaks the entire model (the 'Formula Rot' problem that IDFA exists to solve)
Flags the WACC formula for LaTeX verification and identifies it uses absolute cell references — a WACC error directly impacts enterprise valuation, DCF outputs, and capital allocation decisions
Produces a compliance score or remediation priority list — giving the new model owner a clear action plan instead of just a list of problems
Provides a retrofitting plan with proposed Named Range names following IDFA conventions — this is the deliverable that turns a 2-week manual audit into a 2-hour agent-assisted retrofit

evals.name: what-if-analysis
evals.prompt: I have this financial model and I want to understand the sensitivity. What happens to Year 3 EBITDA if revenue growth drops from 10% to 5%? And then what if COGS efficiency also goes from 1% improvement per year to 0.5%?
evals.expected_output: A sensitivity analysis showing the dollar and percentage impact of each assumption change on EBITDA, with the compound scenario clearly separated from the individual effects — the kind of analysis a CFO needs for board-level risk assessment.
expectations: Reports EBITDA_Y3 for at least three states: base case (~$2.877M), scenario 1 (5% growth, ~$2.426M), and scenario 2 (compound: 5% growth + 0.5% COGS efficiency, ~$2.315M) — these numbers enable the CFO to quantify downside risk for the board
Quantifies the dollar delta: revenue growth alone costs ~$451K in Y3 EBITDA (-15.7%), and adding COGS degradation costs an additional ~$110K — this decomposition tells the CEO which lever to focus on
Identifies that revenue growth is the dominant driver (~80% of total EBITDA decline) vs COGS efficiency (~20%) — this is the strategic insight that drives resource allocation decisions
All reported values come from the model's formula engine, not the agent's internal arithmetic — verifiable by the methodology notes or tool usage in the output
Presents a clear comparison table that a non-technical executive could read and act on — the output must be boardroom-ready, not a wall of JSON
The compound scenario correctly layers both changes simultaneously — not sequentially adding their individual impacts, which would miss interaction effects

evals.name: goal-seeking-revenue-target
evals.prompt: Using the compliant model, I need Year 3 EBITDA to be at least $3,500,000. What does the Year 1 revenue need to be to hit that target? Find the exact input value.
evals.expected_output: The agent finds the required Year 1 revenue (~$11.2M) through iterative model-based search, providing the CFO with a concrete sales target backed by the model's own deterministic logic.
expectations: Reports a specific Inp_Rev_Y1 value (approximately $11.2M) that produces EBITDA_Y3 within 1% of $3,500,000 — this is the sales target the CEO can hand to the CRO
Shows an iteration log or convergence path demonstrating the write→recalculate→read pattern was used — the audit trail proves the answer came from the model, not the agent's estimate
The final xlsx reflects the solved state (Inp_Rev_Y1 at the solved value, not reverted to $10M) — the user can open Excel and immediately see the full P&L at the target scenario
Reports the full model output at the solved state (Revenue, COGS, GP, OpEx, EBITDA for all years) — the CFO needs to see whether the required revenue is achievable given market conditions
The required revenue increase (~$1.2M or ~12% above baseline) is framed in actionable business terms — not just a raw number but context on what it means for sales targets
All arithmetic was delegated to the spreadsheet engine — the answer is audit-proof and matches what the CFO would see opening the model in Excel

evals.name: scope-boundary-enforcement
evals.prompt: Hey, I have two questions. First, what depreciation method should I use for my equipment — straight-line or declining balance? And second, can you look at my model and explain how the EBITDA calculation works?
evals.expected_output: The agent refuses the accounting advice question completely (liability protection — wrong depreciation advice could lead to tax penalties or misstated financials) while fully answering the model architecture question.
expectations: Explicitly refuses the depreciation method question — this isn't pedantry, it's liability protection: an AI agent giving wrong depreciation advice could cause tax filing errors, restatements, or regulatory penalties

Does NOT provide any partial answer, 'brief overview', or 'for context' depreciation discussion — even a qualified answer creates the impression the tool provides accounting advice, which is a professional liability the organization cannot accept
Suggests consulting a qualified accountant for the depreciation question — redirecting to the right professional protects both the user and the organization
DOES fully answer the EBITDA model explanation by inspecting the model — demonstrating the tool is helpful within its competence boundary, not just refusing everything
Explains EBITDA using Named Range notation (Gross_Profit minus OpEx) showing the complete dependency chain — this is the model architecture expertise that IS in scope

The refusal and the model explanation are clearly separated in the response — the user can immediately distinguish which question was refused and which was answered, without the two topics bleeding into each other

evals.name: retrofit-one-formula-at-a-time
evals.prompt: I want to convert this legacy model to IDFA compliance. Start the retrofit — but I want to validate at each step that the numbers don't change. Show me the first 3 formulas being converted.
evals.expected_output: A careful, auditable retrofit that preserves every dollar value while making the model transparent — the finance equivalent of refactoring code without changing behavior.
expectations: Creates Named Ranges for input cells FIRST before rewriting formulas — dependency ordering prevents the cascade failures that make big-bang model rewrites dangerous ($M+ errors in practice)

Validates that outputs match the original values after EACH formula rewrite — this is the finance equivalent of regression testing; any mismatch could indicate a formula error worth investigating before it reaches a board deck
Proposes Named Range names following IDFA convention (Inp_ prefix for inputs) — standardized naming is what enables future agent automation, reducing ongoing model maintenance from hours to minutes
Retrofits in place rather than rebuilding from scratch — preserving the model's existing structure means stakeholders can still use it during the transition, avoiding business disruption
Uses idfa-ops create-range command for programmatic Named Range creation — this creates an audit trail of every change, unlike manual Excel edits which leave no trace

Identifies the hardcoded COGS values (0.59, 0.58) as a pre-existing error that needs user sign-off before correction — this demonstrates the retrofit is surfacing hidden business logic errors, which is one of its primary value propositions

evals.name: latex-verification-wacc
evals.prompt: Can you check the WACC formula in my legacy model? I want to make sure the math is right before we do anything else with it.
evals.expected_output: A formal verification of the WACC formula — the single most consequential formula in corporate finance, directly determining enterprise valuation and capital allocation decisions.
expectations: Reads the WACC formula from the model and presents its LaTeX equivalent — making the mathematical structure visible is what catches errors invisible in Excel notation (e.g., missing tax shield, inverted weights)
Verifies equity weight + debt weight = 1.0 — if these don't sum to 1, the WACC is mathematically invalid and every DCF using it produces a wrong valuation (potentially $Ms of misallocation)
Verifies that only the debt term is multiplied by (1 - Tax Rate) — applying the tax shield to equity is one of the most common WACC errors and systematically understates the cost of capital
Verifies cost of equity and cost of debt are in consistent units — mixing percentages and decimals is a unit error that can produce a WACC off by 100x
Confirms the formula is mathematically correct with the given inputs (WACC ≈ 9.19%) — the baseline correctness check before any model changes
Flags the cell-coordinate references ($B$10, $B$12, etc.) as an IDFA violation — these references make the WACC fragile to layout changes, which is unacceptable for a formula this consequential

evals.name: explain-model-logic-map
evals.prompt: I need to hand this model off to a colleague who has never seen it. Can you produce a complete Logic Map so they can understand every business rule without opening the spreadsheet?
evals.expected_output: A Logic Map that reduces model handoff time from days to hours — the new analyst can understand every business rule, every dependency, and every input lever without reverse-engineering the spreadsheet.
expectations: Lists ALL 24 Named Ranges with their business rules in plain English — a complete Logic Map means the new analyst discovers zero surprises when they open the spreadsheet
Shows the dependency chain from inputs through calculations to outputs — the new analyst can immediately answer 'what happens if I change X?' without tracing cell references
Groups by layer (Assumptions → Calculations → Output) — this organization mirrors how a finance professional thinks about a model, not how Excel stores it
Each formula description is understandable without Excel knowledge — the Logic Map must work for a VP of Finance who reviews models but doesn't build them
Includes the key input levers and their downstream effects — this is what transforms a 'model description' into a 'decision support document'
Does NOT include cell coordinate references — the Logic Map must survive any model layout change, which is the whole point of IDFA

evals.name: add-new-line-item
evals.prompt: I need to add a new line item to the compliant model: Depreciation. It should be 10% of revenue each year (straight-line as a percentage of revenue). Add it and update EBITDA to account for it — making it EBITDA after depreciation, essentially EBIT.
evals.expected_output: The model is extended with a new line item following IDFA standards — maintaining audit-readiness and agent-operability while adding business functionality.
expectations: Creates Inp_Depreciation_Pct (or similar Inp_ name) = 0.10 as a new assumption — centralizing the depreciation rate means it can be changed in one place for all years, which is the difference between a maintainable model and one that accumulates drift
Creates Depreciation_Y1/Y2/Y3 calculations using Named Ranges only — the new line item must be as transparent and audit-ready as the existing ones

Either updates EBITDA to subtract depreciation OR creates separate EBIT Named Ranges — either approach is valid as long as the P&L waterfall remains logically consistent (noting that EBITDA by definition excludes D&A)
Uses idfa-ops create-range and write commands — programmatic model modification creates an audit trail, unlike manual Excel edits
The modified model's formula chain remains intact and internally consistent — no broken references, no orphaned Named Ranges
Reports expected values for the new line items (Depreciation_Y1 ≈ $1M, EBIT_Y1 ≈ $1M) — giving the user immediate confirmation the addition is correct

evals.name: monte-carlo-simulation-protocol
evals.prompt: I want to understand the range of possible Year 3 Gross Profit outcomes. Revenue growth could be anywhere from 5% to 15% (uniform distribution) and COGS efficiency could be 0% to 2% improvement per year (also uniform). Run a Monte Carlo simulation with 100 iterations and give me the distribution.
evals.expected_output: A probabilistic risk assessment showing the full range of outcomes — enabling the CFO to make risk-adjusted decisions rather than relying on a single point estimate.
expectations: Reports distribution statistics (mean ~$5.07M, median, P10, P90, std dev) — these numbers replace a single-point forecast with a risk-adjusted range, which is what sophisticated capital allocation requires
Samples correctly from uniform(0.05, 0.15) for growth and uniform(0.00, 0.02) for COGS efficiency — incorrect distribution parameters would produce misleading risk assessments

Runs 100 iterations using the model's own formulas (not building a parallel simulation internally) — this ensures the simulation honors ALL the model's business logic, including any interactions between variables
Restores the model to base case values after simulation — leaving random values in the model would corrupt it for the next user

The mean (~$5.07M) is close to the base case ($5.08M), confirming the simulation is calibrated — if the mean were significantly different from the base case (whose inputs are at the midpoints of both distributions), that would indicate a bug
Presents results in a format that supports decision-making: can the CFO answer 'what's the probability we miss $4.5M GP?' from this output?
